# Independent code review iteration

Scope: independent full pass over the `octomation/go-tool` template at `07077ca` (2023-12-28): CLI code/tests, initializer scripts, module/tool metadata, Make/Task automation, CI, docs and release workflows, local action, GoReleaser configuration, and public documentation. Existing reviews were not used as input; overlap with prior reviews is intentional independent corroboration. Offline issue and roadmap dumps were excluded.

Validation: on Go 1.26.5, `GOFLAGS=-mod=readonly go test -race ./...` and `GOFLAGS=-mod=readonly go vet ./...` passed. Coverage is 100% for `main` and `internal/command`, but 0% for all demo commands and no tests for configuration. The workflow inventory confirms that `.github/workflows/cd.dist.yml` does not exist.

Severity: **Crit** / **High** / **Med** / **Low** / **Nit**.
Class: `correctness` · `security` · `concurrency` · `perf` · `api` · `idiom` · `modern` · `test` · `build` · `docs`.

## 1. CI calls a reusable release workflow that is absent

The `release` job references `./.github/workflows/cd.dist.yml`, but the repository contains only `cd.yml` and `cd.docs.yml`. Local reusable workflows must resolve to an existing file with a `workflow_call` trigger. `cd.yml` is not a drop-in replacement because it has no `workflow_call` event and independently triggers on tags.

At best the release job cannot run; GitHub may reject the calling workflow during validation, taking lint/test with it. Meanwhile tag pushes also start `cd.yml`, leaving two contradictory release designs.

**Fix.** Choose one path. Either delete the `release` job and keep a single tag-triggered `cd.yml`, or create `cd.dist.yml` with `workflow_call`, declare secrets/permissions, and remove the duplicate tag trigger. Validate the workflow before propagating the template.

## 2. Template initialization leaves upstream release identities intact

The advertised `run init my.new/tool` implementation changes only `go.mod` and Go import strings. It does not replace `%template%` in Cobra help, `project_name: tool`, `release.github.owner/name: octomation/go-tool`, Homebrew metadata, install URLs, docs, badges, Docker/repository identities, or the binary name. A repository generated from the template can compile successfully yet attempt to publish into the upstream `octomation/go-tool` project, normally failing only at release time.

**Fix.** Make initialization a transactional renderer with explicit owner/repository/module/binary/display-name inputs. Maintain a manifest of templated files, fail if placeholders or upstream identifiers remain, and test generation in a temporary repository through build, help output, snapshot release, and docs build.

## 3. Event messages are interpolated into executable shell

The notify action places `${{ github.event.head_commit.message }}`, dispatch reasons, and workflow names directly inside a Bash heredoc. Expressions are expanded before shell parsing, so crafted message content can terminate the heredoc or introduce command substitution. Jobs using the action carry the Slack webhook and, for releases/docs, additional permissions or secrets.

**Fix.** Put event data in environment variables and treat it strictly as data when writing `$GITHUB_OUTPUT`; do not interpolate untrusted context properties into `run:` scripts. Reference: [GitHub script-injection guidance](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/security/script-injections).

## 4. Pull requests can deploy the production documentation site

After tests, CI unconditionally invokes `cd.docs.yml` with Pages write and OIDC permissions. That includes `pull_request` events. The reusable workflow checks out the PR revision and calls `deploy-pages` in the production `github-pages` environment, so a passing contributor change can replace public docs unless an out-of-repository environment rule blocks it.

**Fix.** On pull requests build a permissionless preview artifact only. Deploy solely from a protected main-branch push after lint and tests, with an explicit environment approval if desired.

## 5. Dependency-only updates bypass CI

The path filter uses the literal `go.{mod,sum}`. GitHub path globs do not implement brace expansion; neither actual module file matches. Dependabot changes can therefore merge without compilation, tests, linting, vulnerability checks, or release validation.

**Fix.** List `go.mod` and `go.sum` explicitly and include `tools/go.mod`, `tools/go.sum`, release files, and docs lockfiles in their owning gates. Reference: [GitHub path pattern syntax](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-syntax#patterns-to-match-file-paths).

## 6. The release engine floats independently of its legacy configuration

The release workflow downloads `version: latest`, while `.goreleaser.yml` has no `version: 2` header and uses v1-era fields. The tools module separately pins GoReleaser 1.23.0, so local checks and release CI intentionally run different major lines. A future/latest change can break tags with no repository diff.

**Fix.** Migrate and test the configuration under a pinned GoReleaser v2, add `version: 2`, use the same version locally and in CI, and run `goreleaser check` plus a snapshot before tag creation. Reference: [GoReleaser version errors](https://www.goreleaser.com/resources/errors/version/).

## 7. The fast compile check executes all tests

`go-check` passes `-test.run=^$(uuidgen)`. Because `uuidgen` is not a Make variable, it expands to empty and the regexp becomes `^`, matching every test. CI then runs the entire suite again in `make test`. Derived tools with stateful or expensive tests pay twice and can observe conflicting side effects.

**Fix.** Use `go test -run='^$' ./...` for compile-only validation, or remove the duplicate stage.

## 8. A new CLI template starts on a stale ecosystem baseline

The root module is Go 1.20; tools are Go 1.21 with 2023 versions and personal forks (`golangci-lint` 1.55, GoReleaser 1.23, `golang/mock` 1.6). CI stops explicit testing at 1.21 plus floating latest. For a project generator, this bakes migration debt into every new repository and omits current supply-chain controls such as pinned actions and artifact provenance.

**Fix.** Raise the generated support floor deliberately, migrate tooling/config together, replace archived mock tooling, pin Actions and release tools, and test one freshly generated repository rather than only the template source.

## 9. Generated applications ship with an intentional panic command

`command.New` unconditionally registers `demo.Panic()`, alongside stdout/stderr demos. Unless users manually delete it, every generated production CLI exposes a public subcommand whose sole behavior is to panic on user-provided text. Recovery turns it into exit 1, but shipping a deliberate crash path is surprising API surface and can pollute error telemetry.

**Fix.** Move demos into examples or guard them behind a build tag explicitly excluded from generated production code. The starter CLI should contain one harmless domain placeholder, not fault-injection commands.

## 10. Public update and docs targets report success while doing nothing

The README advertises `make` workflows, yet `update` and `docs` are `@true` with TODO comments. Automation and callers receive exit 0 and cannot distinguish an implemented operation from a stub. In a template, silent no-ops propagate into every downstream project.

**Fix.** Implement the targets or fail clearly with an explanatory non-zero status. Remove them from help/docs until they have executable behavior.

## 11. The fuzz target consumes a package as its regexp

`go test -fuzz $(PACKAGES)` gives the first package token to the required `-fuzz` regexp option. It does not select that package as intended, and there are no fuzz targets in the template.

**Fix.** Add a real fuzz test and invoke `go test ./... -fuzz="$(FUZZ)" -fuzztime=...`, or remove the misleading target.

## 12. Source installation uses go get for a binary

The README recommends `go get go.octolab.org/template/tool@latest`. Modern Go uses `go install path@version` for executables; `go get` edits the current module's dependencies.

**Fix.** Use `go install`, specify the supported Go version, and validate the command in a clean temporary module during release CI.
